 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_DIAGNOSTIC: In some GUIs known `Debug`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_INFORMATIONAL: In some GUIs known as `Info` or `Information`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_LOW: In some GUIs known `Warning`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_MEDIUM: In some GUIs known as `Error` or `Threat`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_HIGH: In some GUIs known as `Critical`

get.description:
Returns a list of incidents matching the `filter`.
The list is ordered according to `order_by`.

parameters.name: filter
parameters.description:
A filter string compliant with [Filtering](https://google.aip.dev/160).
Field names must be expressed in lower_case_with_underscores form or in lowerCamelCase form, for example, display_name or displayName.
For time filtering, use the timestamp offset representation.

Examples:
- incident.create_time >= timestamp("2024-10-01T12:41:39+00:00")
- incident.assignee_uuid == "uuid"
- incident.displayName.contains("abc")
- displayName.contains("abc")

> **Info:**
> For more details about filtering, see [ESET Connect Online Help](https://help.eset.com/eset_connect/en-US/incident_management_v2_incidents_get.html#s-filtering)
> Common Expression Language is defined at [GitHub: Google Open Source](https://github.com/google/cel-spec/blob/master/doc/intro.md) along with libraries for
[C++/C#](https://github.com/google/cel-go), [Go](https://github.com/google/cel-go)

parameters.name: orderBy
parameters.description:
It is a comma-separated list of fields. The default sorting order is ascending. To specify the descending order for a field, users must append a " desc" suffix.
All actual fields and subfields from the object [Incident] can be used. Subfields are specified with a . character.
Field names must be expressed in lower_case_with_underscores form, for example, display_name.

> **Info:**
> Modeled after: [Standard Methods: List Ordering](https://google.aip.dev/132#ordering)
> For more details about ordering, see [ESET Connect Online Help](https://help.eset.com/eset_connect/en-US/incident_management_v2_incidents_get.html#s-ordering)

For example, the result will be ordered by the device name given by the user and by the incident severity in the reverse order:
display_name, severity desc

parameters.name: pageSize
parameters.description:
Limit for pagination purposes.

If unspecified or 0, the default value is 50. The maximum value is 1000; values above 1000 will be coerced to 1000.

> **Info:**

Severity levels abstracted to cover all the possible GUIs. Vocabulary is leaving interpretation of severity level completely to API client.

> **info:**
> This approach is inevitable on the SIEM level as many contributing sources exist. Keeping the local names for severity levels never fits all the GUIs.

 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_DIAGNOSTIC: In some GUIs known as `Debug`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_INFORMATIONAL: In some GUIs known as `Info` or `Information`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_LOW: In some GUIs known as `Warning`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_MEDIUM: In some GUIs known as `Error` or `Threat`
 - SEVERITY_LEVEL_HIGH: In some GUIs known as `Critical`

emailReference.description:
Reference to the affected email. Emails can be sourced in various systems with different types of indexing (for example, URI, UUID, etc.).

Object reference must be unique within one parent object (for example, email or archive) to ensure referential integrity or the scope of protection (for example, one account).

For example:
- Outlook email can be referenced by EntryID (or SearchKey)
- Exchange email can be referenced by the combination of `Timestamp` | `Subject` | `Sender` fields.

> **Info:**
> Optimally, the reference should be a universally unique identifier. That prevents from ambiguities during pairing of the object with scan results with actions. 
> 
> This might, however, be substantially difficult to achieve in the distributed email system, where the id-issuing authority is out of our reach and might be spoofed by, for example, spammers.

fileReference.description:
Reference to the file affected by the response. Files can be hosted in various systems with different types of indexing (for example, URI, UUID, etc.).

Object reference must be unique within one parent object (for example, email or archive) to ensure referential integrity.

Optimally, the reference should be a universally unique identifier. That prevents from ambiguities during pairing of the object with scan results with actions.

> **Info:**
> There might be multiple responses linked to some of the troublesome files. For example, an email with the infected attachment might get that attachment quarantined.

storages.description:
An array that represents the various storage locations associated with a file.

Each element in the array is an object that represents a different storage location. This could include email, archive, Microsoft Sharepoint site, Hyper-V Volume, etc. 

The order of the elements in the array represents the hierarchy of storage locations. For example, a storages array of ["email", "archive"] indicates that the file is first associated with an email, and within that email, it’s stored in an archive.

Principal responsible for the revision of the entity. It might be the identification of the user.

Every revision might have a different editor. For non-revisioned entities, the editor denotes the author of the last revision.

For just-created entities, `author` and `editor` are the same.

v2EdrRule.description:
[EDR rule] where actions are executed based on the criteria.

[EDR rule] defines one or more actions executed as a result of suspicious activity.

displayName.description:
Human-readable name of the [EDR rule exclusion].

The value is derived from the `description/name` value in `xml_definition`.

enabled.description: If `true`, the exclusion will be applied.

ruleUuids.description:
Reference to the [automation rules] for which the exclusion applies.

type: EdrRule

scopes.description:
List of scopes in which the rule exclusion is used.

For example, a `rule exclusion` is scoped to `device_uuid`; it would never be triggered for events from devices other than `device_uuid`.

One rule exclusion might be applicable in multiple scopes.

Rule exclusion can also have scopes disjunct to the rules it excludes. In this case, the exclusion is never applied. This status cannot be prevented as the rules might change scopes over time regardless of the rule exclusion.

xmlDefinition.description:
XML definition of the [EDR rule exclusion].

Specification of the format is the same as for [automation rule](https://help.eset.com/ei_rules/latest/en-US/), but actions are ignored.

XML definition must be valid according to this specification for the [EDR rule exclusion] to be valid.

uuid.description:
Unique identifier of the entity.

Must be collision-free - two identifiers created anywhere in the world must not collide within entity parent scope. Unless a member of aggregate, the entity scope is always global.

Although most of the times compliant with [RFC 4122: A Universally Unique IDentifier (UUID) URN Namespace](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4122), do not rely on it being a RFC UUID. Treat it as an opaque identifier.

RFC UUID can be recognized by being formatted according to the template xxxxxxxx-xxxx-Mxxx-Nxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx, as explained on [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universally_unique_identifier#Format).

UUID is used for referencing an entity, even across domains.

Example: "123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000"

authorUuid.description: Principal responsible for the first revision of the entity. It might be the identification of the user.

editorUuid.description:
Principal responsible for the revision of the entity. It might be the identification of the user.

Every revision might have a different editor. For non-revisioned entities, the editor denotes the author of the last revision.

For just-created entities, `author` and `editor` are the same.

v2EdrRuleExclusion.description:
[EDR rule exclusion] patches one or more [EDR rule]s so that they do not perform their action when spotting a suspicious activity.

Exclusions are defined in the same way as rules, except their actions are ignored.

deviceUuid.description:
Reference to the device for which the rule is applicable.

type: device_management.v1.Device

deviceGroupUuid.description:
Reference to the `device_group` for which the rule is applicable.

type: device_management.v1.DeviceGroup

v2EdrRuleScope.description: Scope for which the rule (exclusion) is applicable.

localIpAddress.description: The IP address of endpoint-local network interface.

localMacAddress.description: The MAC (L2) address of endpoint-local network interface.

localPort.description: The port on the endpoint side of the communication.

protocolKeyword.description:
Network protocol used in the communication.

Name must be from `keyword` column of the [IANA list](https://www.iana.org/assignments/protocol-numbers/protocol-numbers.xhtml)

remoteIpAddress.description: The IP address of the network interface of the remote host.

remoteMacAddress.description: The MAC (L2) address of the remote network interface (possibly the MAC address of the gateway).

remotePort.description: The port on the remote side of the communication.

v2EndpointNetworkTraffic.description:
Descriptor of an endpoint `network communication` involved in the (indicated) incident.

For endpoint scenarios, it is useful to identify the endpoint side as `local`, while the other end of network communication as `remote`.

v2Incident.description:
Incidents: An incident is a confirmed or verified security event that has been investigated and determined to be a genuine security concern. Incidents are not necessarily a one-to-one mapping with individual security events. Instead, they are the result of the analysis and validation of one or more security events that collectively indicate a security breach or a significant security incident.

Incident: When the security events are confirmed as genuine security incidents, they are classified as incidents. Incidents require a focused response, including containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident analysis.

[Security Event Logs](https://www.mezmo.com/learn-logging-for-devops/what-are-security-event-logs)
A security incident is a security event that has escalated to a compromise. Returning to the example, assume a cybercriminal has stolen the user’s login credentials and successfully used them to access the application.

Upon discovering an incident, your IT department or managed security vendor would initiate an incident response and remediation protocol, taking fast action to contain data and downtime losses using their various tools and skills.

[Security Event vs Security Incident](https://simplicable.com/IT/security-event-vs-security-incident)
An event that is a viable [risk](https://simplicable.com/risk/risk) or that causes damage, such as lost data or operational disruptions.

v2IncidentComment.description:
Incident comment is every comment connected to an incident.
These comments are created during the life cycle of the incident and better describe what happened to the incident during each step of its life cycle (Preparation, Detection, Analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-incident activity).

deviceCount.description: Count of devices related to the incident.

executableCount.description: Count of executables related to the incident.

processCount.description: Count of processes related to the incident.

v2IncidentResolveReason.description:
Possible reasons for resolved incident.

 - INCIDENT_RESOLVE_REASON_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - INCIDENT_RESOLVE_REASON_TRUE_POSITIVE: The incident was a true positive, indicating a genuine security threat.
 - INCIDENT_RESOLVE_REASON_FALSE_POSITIVE: The incident was initially thought to be a security threat but later determined to be a false alarm.
 - INCIDENT_RESOLVE_REASON_SUSPICIOUS: The incident is not a confirmed threat (true positive), but investigating it can provide valuable insights into user behavior patterns and help mitigate potential attacks.

v2IncidentSeverityLevel.description:
Severity levels abstracted to cover all the possible GUIs. Vocabulary is leaving interpretation of severity level completely to API client.

> **Info:**
>  This approach is inevitable on SIEM level as there are many contributing sources. Keeping the local names for severity levels never fits all the GUIs.

 - INCIDENT_SEVERITY_LEVEL_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - INCIDENT_SEVERITY_LEVEL_LOW: In some GUIs known as `Warning`
 - INCIDENT_SEVERITY_LEVEL_MEDIUM: In some GUIs known as `Error` or `Threat`
 - INCIDENT_SEVERITY_LEVEL_HIGH: In some GUIs known as `Critical`

v2IncidentStatus.description:
Defines an enumeration for incident status.

 - INCIDENT_STATUS_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - INCIDENT_STATUS_OPEN: The incident is OPEN (also known as NEW) and has been reported or detected.
 - INCIDENT_STATUS_IN_PROGRESS: The incident is currently in progress and being actively addressed.
 - INCIDENT_STATUS_CLOSED: The incident has been closed, and the necessary actions have been taken.
 - INCIDENT_STATUS_WAITING_FOR_INPUT: The incident is awaiting input from the customer.

v2NetworkTrafficDirection.description:
Direction of network communication.

 - NETWORK_TRAFFIC_DIRECTION_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - NETWORK_TRAFFIC_DIRECTION_INBOUND: The direction from remote host towards local host (the device where detection occurred).
 - NETWORK_TRAFFIC_DIRECTION_OUTBOUND: The direction from local host (the device where detection occurred) towards remote host.

v2ObjectActionType.description:
Categories of operations that can be performed on objects.

 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED: fallback
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_NO_ACTION: No action was done.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_ERROR: Action was not performed because of error.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_QUARANTINE: Class of quarantining actions.

For example: 
- attachment quarantined
- email quarantined
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_CLEAN: Class of cleaning actions.

For example when a malware is removed from zip archive.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_DELETE: Class of deleting actions.

For example:
- email deleted
- file deleted
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_REPLACE: Class of replacing actions.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_MOVE_TO_FOLDER: Actions where object is moved to a certain folder.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_MOVE_TO_TRASH: Actions where object is moved to trash folder.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_MOVE_TO_JUNK: Actions where object is moved to junk folder.
 - OBJECT_ACTION_TYPE_TERMINATE: Actions where some process is terminated.

For example:
- download terminated
- connection terminated
- process terminated (killed).

filter.description:
Filter supports the following operators `eq`, `ne`, `gt`, `ge`, `lt`, `le`, `and`, `or`, and `()`. Operators can be combined with values to filter data. For instance, `resolved eq 0` will report only unresolved detections.

Nested fields are addressed by using dot notation. For example, `context.userName eq "Hans"`.

> **Info:**
> Filter mimics the semantics [defined by Microsoft](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/openspecs/windows_protocols/ms-odata/7d6c4117-317d-4860-915b-7e321be017e3).
>
> Filter is also a standard field from Google's perspective, despite this one uses non-Google expression language: [Standard Fields](https://google.aip.dev/148)

returnTotalSize.description: If true, the `total_size` of [detection group]s collection is computed for the response.

v2TriggeringEventType.title: Event that triggered the detection
v2TriggeringEventType.description:
- TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED: Fallback
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_STARTED: Process was started
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_TERMINATED: Process was terminated
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_STARTED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION: Process started before data collection
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_ENDED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION: Process ended before data collection
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_EXECUTED: Process executed with exec system call
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_CODE_INJECTED: Some code was injected to the process
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_OPENED: Existing process was opened to have its memory read. 
> **Info:**
> [OpenProcess function](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/processthreadsapi/nf-processthreadsapi-openprocess)
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_FILE_DELETED: File was deleted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_FILE_RENAMED: File was renamed
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_FILE_REWRITTEN: Some data was written to the file
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_UNSAVED_FILE_DELETED_ON_CLOSE: Some process is creating file, but when closed, the file is deleted as it was not requested to save it
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_FILE_TRUNCATED: Some process is opening a file with some data, but it is requested to delete the data
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_TCP_IP_CONNECTED: TCP/IP connection was established
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_TCP_IP_DISCONNECTED: TCP/IP connection was closed
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_TCP_IP_CONNECTION_ACCEPTED: TCP/IP connection was accepted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_REGISTRY_KEY_CREATED: New registry key was created
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_REGISTRY_KEY_DELETED: Existing registry key was deleted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_REGISTRY_KEY_VALUE_SET: New registry key value added/modified
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_REGISTRY_KEY_VALUE_DELETED: Existing registry key value deleted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_HTTP_REQUEST_SENT: Some HTTP request is sent, which can mean that browser is opening some website.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_REGISTRY_KEY_RENAMED: Existing registry key renamed
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_EXECUTABLE_FILE_DROPPED: Executable file was written to the disk
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SHARED_LIBRARY_LOADED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION: Some shared DLL was loaded before data collection started

DEPRECATED in favor of `LIBRARY_LOADED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION`.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_LIBRARY_LOADED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION: Some DLL was loaded before data collection started.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_DYNAMIC_SHARED_LIBRARY_LOADED: Some process loaded DLL
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_DNS_RESOLVED: DNS resolution
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_LOGGED_IN: User logged in
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_LOGGED_OUT: User logged out
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_ACTIVATED: Disabled user was activated
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_DISABLED: Active user was disabled
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_CREATED: New user was created
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_DELETED: User was deleted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_ADDED_TO_GROUP: User was added to specific group
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_REMOVED_FROM_GROUP: User was removed from specific group
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_AMSI_TRIGGERED: AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) was exeuted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WMI_AUTO_EXECUTED_PERSISTENCE: WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) persistence on execution. This means that a specific piece of code or a script continues to execute or re-executes even after a system restart.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WMI_EXECUTED_PROCESS: Process was started or triggered by Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI).
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WMI_QUERY_DISPLAYED: WMI query has been executed. This might occur in a system monitoring tool, a script, or a log where the output of a WMI query is shown to the user or recorded for review.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_MONITORED_FILE_OPENED: Opening file for reading = triggered when a monitored file was read. Monitored files refer to those which contain either sensitive information or stored credentials.
For example, stored browser passwords, stored FTP clients passwords, AD database and so on.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PROCESS_COMMUNICATION_USED_NAMED_PIPE: Some process is trying to communicate with another process using the named pipes
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_TCP_IP_NETWORK_PROTOCOL_IDENTIFIED: Info received from Firewall about the network protocols of the identified TCP connection. For example: IMAP, POP3, RDP, RMI.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_DRIVER_LOADED: Driver was loaded
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_DRIVER_UNLOADED: Driver was unloaded
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_FILE_MARKED_EXECUTABLE: File was marked as executable
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SYSTEM_CALLED_API: Monitoring API calls commonly used by malware
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_MULTIPLE_FILES_CHANGED: Information from RansomWare Shield, that something changed multiple files in short time range
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WINDOWS_SERVICE_INSTALLED: Windows service has been installed
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WINDOWS_SERVICE_STARTED: Windows service started
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WINDOWS_SERVICE_MODIFIED: Windows service has been changed or modified
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WINDOWS_SERVICE_STOPPED: Windows service stopped
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_WINDOWS_SERVICE_REMOVED: Windows service has been removed or uninstalled
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SCHEDULED_TASK_ADDED: Scheduled task has been added
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SCHEDULED_TASK_STARTED: Scheduled task started
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SCHEDULED_TASK_MODIFIED: Scheduled task has been changed or modified
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_SCHEDULED_TASK_REMOVED: Scheduled task has been removed
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_VIRTUAL_DISK_MOUNTED: A virtual disk has been mounted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_BITS_COPY_JOB_FILE_ADDED: A file has been added to a [BITS copy job](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/bits/ne-bits-bg_job_type)
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_VOLUME_SHADOW_COPY_DELETED: Volume shadow copy was deleted
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_LOGGED_IN_REMOTELY: User logged in remotely
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_USER_LOGGED_OUT_REMOTELY: User logged out of the remote connection
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PNP_DEVICE_CONNECTED: A [plug and play](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plug_and_play#Current_plug_and_play_interfaces) device has been plugged.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_PNP_DEVICE_DISCONNECTED: A [plug and play](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plug_and_play#Current_plug_and_play_interfaces) device has been unplugged.
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_CHILD_PROCESS_STARTED: Child process was created
 - TRIGGERING_EVENT_TYPE_CHILD_PROCESS_STARTED_BEFORE_DATA_COLLECTION: Child process was started before data collection started
